turns.text: I haven't read that opinion. I have sent words, but I assume it's not too difficult to see the process by which the Court arrived to that inasmuch as a man may come in and pay $623. He comes in and says, “This is my property. That's a fund. Would you translate that fund into dollars and cents and take out what belongs to you and give the rest to me?”

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That he has a right to do that under the law.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Under the Chapman Docks case, he clearly has.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, is there any basis upon which the City could refuse him if he asks that -- makes that request?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: The City could not refuse to accept the payment of the taxes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: No, I mean if he asks that the -- the separate parcels be separately sold. The City take what's owing it and to pay him the balance maybe the City refused to do that.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Well, before the Chapman Docks case, we apparently thought that we could refuse.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: No, not -- not -- we --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: -- we did not appeal with Chapman Docks case and as the Chapman Docks case construes the law, we cannot now refuse (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, that -- that's absolute right that the taxpayer had.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, under the present statute.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Had that procedure ever been opened to -- to the appellant here?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Had -- yes. Yes, indeed, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Would it have had to be before the -- the Docks case?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Well, the Docks case was not decided until this year.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: He could have done exactly what the property owner in the Chapman Docks case did and presumably, the result had it been litigated would have been the same.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That what --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: So, there was no knowledge that they -- on -- on the part of the public generally that they had that right. That -- that is really the initial statute, wasn't it?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Well, I would say this that there was some doubt as to what the statute meant before the Chapman Docks case and that doubt has been resolved by the decision therein. But you see, Your Honor, in this case, the trouble again was the bookkeeper.He just pocketed these notices of before of the in rem foreclosure and the trustee says that he never got the notices. And, of course, we accept that statement as true, but now however unfortunate that result may be. So far as the question of due process is concerned, it's difficult to see what more we could have done here. You just can't show a personal service which has never been told to be a prerequisite to due process in an in rem foreclosure suit. There are all kinds of tax statutes where you get judgments against property, all sorts of instances of notices by publication, which is the only notice, which have been held to satisfy the requirements of due process. Now, short of actually personally serving these trustees, it's difficult to see what more we could have done in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, you offered the Chapman Docks doctrine as I understand it as a (Inaudible) his contention that you provide for on automatic taking of his property as a whole, irrespective of the value of the property?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And that still to use the question of the notice to the bookkeeper.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I notice in as I now, fairly rapidly looked at the small type in the Chapman Docks opinion and I notice they say that if a party says and so on, the Court should inquire as the case of the proper for directly of sale so that surplus moneys may be available to the answering party. And I noticed they were confronted with the -- the Village of Mamaroneck -- Mamaroneck case 273 App. Div. (Inaudible) Anyhow, they have that opinion staring them in the face and they distinguished that case because they said in that case the owner -- merely the owner didn't say the property was worth more. He didn't claim a sale. He didn't claim a surplus interest so that presumably this is a moot and has been a mooted question in New York and then they go on and say, “the -- if it -- it be intended that as an owner who has answer and property has a value substantially exceeding the amount of the tax liens is to be deprived of an equitable right to surplus money, the statute must so state or so indicate clearly.” And their decision is that they impliedly read (Inaudible) of provision into the statute.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is that a fair construction of (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: I -- I think it is. And I think that answers the constitutional objections which the appellant has to the case.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How many in value does the Appellate Division have there, about 300?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Well, they -- they got up I think to --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was it 300?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: No, they -- they got up to 286 and then they jumped into 1 A.D.2d.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: My associate advises me that 273 App. Div. is 1948, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, if -- that preceded this litigation.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Oh, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That preceded this litigation and this question --

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Surely.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- this general problem was canvassing the Mamaroneck.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: It was, Your Honor. Oh, yes. I would like to mention one other thing, Your Honor, which I overlooked when I was discussing the question as to whether or not the appeal was moot with respect to the larger parcel. The ameliorative statute vests discretions in the Board of Estimate. However, so far, that -- there have been 191 applications for the return of parcels which were taken. We have granted 157. We have not rejected any. The only reason that the others haven't been granted is that we're still investigating when they're being processed. And in the letters which I'm going to hand up to, Your Honors, there is the statement by the Director of the Bureau of Real Estate that he will recommend to the Board of Estimate that the application be granted. Now, I think that -- that I can assure the Court that this application will be granted. I mean, I wouldn't ask the Court to dismiss the appeal as academic as to that parcel on any theory that the Board of Estimate still had any discretion. We'll --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Do you have discretion beyond the exemption for the statute? (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: I'm talking about discretion to -- discretion to give them back their property after the foreclosure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's the discretion there?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Well, the -- the words say the Board of Estimate in its discretion, but we -- we've granted every application so far and we do not propose to deny this one.I mean, I just --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I ask you this question?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Surely.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It's bearing on the question of mootness. It can be mooted only if the exact, as I see it this is my (Inaudible)

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Go ahead.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- if that which he claimed, that which he's entitled to claim on his own theory is provided by the statute to wit that he gets, that he merely is the subject to the delinquent taxes. Now, does the statute leave him in that, even wholly the new statute?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Now, I -- I cannot in -- in fairness say that because it does provide that he shall pay the cost that has a maximum --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: -- has a maximum limit --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But I -- yes, I don't -- why would the --

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: -- as to cost.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Cost. Well (Inaudible), but beyond that, he -- it gives him what he claims to be -- he was improperly or invalidly, illegally deprived of that.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Well, I want to --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Namely, the surplus value beyond the statutes and clause.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: He gets the property back.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He gets the property back. He simply has to take actions for that.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: That's correct. It -- it doesn't apply to the property that's been sold.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That -- then?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: As to the -- and to the other property.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He gets the property (Inaudible)

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Now, Your Honor, in -- in fairness to Mr. Jones, he does say in his brief that he doesn't want to do that because the way he construes the statute, if he gets the property back he says, “We can keep the rent. We've been collecting rents for three or four years.” I'm not sure that that's the proper construction of the statute. I'm inclined to think it isn't, but that question is not before the Court and I see no point in going --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, is he --

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: --in -- into it --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- would be in the position to litigate it?

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Oh, certainly he would. Indeed, he would. If he pays the $15,000 which represent taxes plus a small -- and it was plus the small amount, of course, he'll get this property back.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Then the question whether he should have had the rental on the City while he owned it (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Is -- is a matter of dispute or possible, I hope not, possible additional litigation. Now, Your Honors, I -- I've tried to outline the procedure here. The only thing I can say in conclusion is that the constitutionality of the statute of this character has to be viewed in the light of the problem involved. We have 834,000 separate parcels of real estate in New York City. And at the time that the affidavits were presented here, we had foreclosed against something like 40,000 for delinquent taxes and I'm advised that we have proceeded against up to date, against something like 57,000 parcels and have acquired title to them. Now, Your Honors must realize also that in many instances, arrears of taxes are relatively small. There is no practicable way of collecting these taxes by the old Title A method, because by the time you or it would take years and years and years to conduct the necessary title searches on all the various parcels involved. We have devised by this method what we think is an expeditious way of collecting these taxes which we think satisfies the requirements of due process.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Pardon me. Do you -- did I understand it correctly that -- that New York City has taken titles of 57,000 --

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: -- pieces of land?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Pieces of real estate.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: -- of real -- of real property have been foreclosed by this in rem method. And since the ameliorative statute was passed which was early this year, there had been 191 applications for the return of properties. I might say that under the ameliorative provision, we may not dispose of the property for a period of four months with reference to the future. And within that four-month period, any owner can come in and make application for the return of the property upon the payment of the back taxes, as was done here in the incidence of the Powell Street property. And Your Honor, may I hand up to the Court the copies of the -- of the letters of --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, you may give to the Court.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: With joy, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Jones.

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: May it please the Court. I'd like to correct, as soon as I can, some misimpressions about the Chapman case that may have -- had left with you. The Chapman case was decided long after this case that we're trying now was decided and long after, of course, this case has begun. Of course, this case also was begun after any opportunity to make use of the answer in this foreclosure action had expired and the -- and the Chapman interpretation of that section is only good for a taxpayer whose time to answer hasn't expired.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But I suggest that in our profession, we assume that when a decision is handed down, it wasn't concocted the minute that the decision was handed down, but I'm not resorting to a fiction as evidence of the lawyer of the Chapman. He thought that the statute was in itself without construction and he was alerted to think so by the discussion of Mamaroneck case.

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: There's one good reason why the one thing I -- I know the lawyer in the Chapman case and -- and the -- and the -- the state statute, Your Honor, that is, this prototype statute of New York State does provide that where an answer is filed. Then, you'll have certain rights that you don't have when you don't file an answer to that. We all knew that very, very clearly and discussed that particular point that the state statute provided that where an answer is filed, you can then, in a proper case, move for a -- to request the sale --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm -- I'm merely suggesting --

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: -- but this -- this -- that you didn't have it there.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I merely suggest that the -- that the stimulus to all that the statute might be so interpreted wasn't there and I should think it is there particularly (Inaudible) that wasn't merely a case in which you could reason on the fact that the Chapman's doctrine isn't -- wasn't really in that case.

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: Well, they can't ever say that the -- this case here was started long before.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Not to my mind. When was this being started?

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: This case was started in January of 1953.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, the Mamaroneck case at 273 App. Div. does not involve after 1948.

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: No, I don't think there is anything into the Mamaroneck case that have stimulated anybody's thought that the -- that -- that filing an answer would have been includable under the relief in this statute, but what good would it have done to anybody in this case? The chance to answer had long since expired, long since.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But there was a chance (Inaudible)

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: No, there wasn't, Your Honor, and that's what I want to correct --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Why not?

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: Because the answer had to be filed 20 days after -- it had to be filed 10 weeks after this foreclosure action and that have gone on years before.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Do you mean because of the -- because of the notion that he knows? It went back to me.

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: At -- at any rate, any chance to make use of an -- of an interpretation of -- of what could be done with an answered file in this case, long since gone.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I suppose we could -- we could pass the old act unconstitutional on the grounds it provided for an automatic taking of your property when it, of course, has held at any time it doesn't make -- it doesn't provide for such conditions, couldn't we?

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: Well, yes, if you -- if you're -- if you have the misfortune to miss your chance to answer --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, that's the question of the adequacy of the note. That's a different question.

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: Well, I mean, then you have a -- then, there is a confiscation if you miss your chance to answer. Just a few brief words on this -- on this remedial statute. Remedial statute says that the taxpayer has to pay not only all of the taxes that were due at the time the City took the property but all of the taxes since that date plus interest during the period of the City's claimed ownership and while the City is collecting the rents. You're given no credit for the rent. In addition to that --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Do you agree you would have an action against the City for those rents in the event that you pay the taxes now?

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: I have -- agree that I'd have an awful fight with the City on the interpretation of that statute --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But you agreed --

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: -- because I -- it has been discussed on the phone who would get those rents and that it has -- I've have been informed that the City is going to keep the rents.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Do you mean that they're legally entitled to them?

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: Under the statute, they claimed that they're entitled to the rent. So, you see there -- it's a fight who's going to get the benefit of about four years of grants which are considerable. So, it's -- it's a very -- it --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And you have a pretty strong argument, wouldn't you, that they're going to make you pay the taxes as though it was your property?

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: Well, you would have --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The City certainly ought to give you the rent. (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: That -- that is certainly a strong argument.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- at the cost of collecting.

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: That is a strong argument if I have to go -- have to fight this one out too, I'll certainly remember what Your Honor has said promptly [Laughter] the strength of the argument.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I hope so.

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: But there should be an end of litigation sometime and I hope that we don't have to go to --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What will you say to the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- one-night decision? Will you say -- will you quote it as a one-night decision of the City?

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: [Laughter]

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Nobody dissented yet.

turns.name: WILLIAM P. JONES
turns.text: I have only a few minutes left and I would like to point out something that I didn't get to in my main argument. That when the City resorted to this stringent method of collecting the taxes, there was available to it existing in as part of their tax law, the method under Title A which was the well-accepted method of selling a tax lien. And then, the purchaser of the tax lien forecloses the tax lien and the owner gets surplus. Now, that method was available and it was an alternative method. This wasn't a mandatory method in rem. They had a choice. They had an alternative. But they chose to use the -- the abrupt, the confiscatory method because the -- the record undoubtedly showed here that this taxpayer didn't know what was going on and that this was the -- a sure windfall that was brought in to the in rem proceeding which it turned out to be some $51,000 of windfall property. Also, there's the clearly expressed intent of the legislature contained in the Governor's Bill Jacket which says that the in rem method was intended to get back on the tax rules, heavily encumbered properties whose -- where the taxes had -- had practically doubled up the economic value. And that Title A, this is the corresponding title in the state statute, was intended to be applied to improve properties. Now, I -- I submit that the -- that under the circumstances of this case that the action of the City violated the appellant's constitutional rights. It isn't necessary to knock out the entire statute. We claim that only as applied to the appellants in this case. However, it would be within the scope of the Court if they saw so fit. We urge that the order be reversed with cause.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: Excuse -- excuse me, Your Honor. The -- I find that the statute has been amended in a minor respect, not relevant really to the case here, but may I leave the amendment with the Court?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, you may.

turns.name: SEYMOUR B. QUEL
turns.text: With joy. Thank you.
